hypothesis: libp2p requires a tokio async runtime; Bevy owns its own task scheduler and is not tokio-native. Bridging them causes thread-pool contention, executor conflicts, and non-deterministic frame timing.
evidence: Bevy 0.13 uses its own bevy_tasks executor (based on async-executor, NOT tokio). libp2p 0.53+ hard-depends on tokio::runtime::Runtime.
Running a second tokio Runtime inside a Bevy app is legal but means two thread pools competing for CPU cores on the same process.
libp2p network events must be polled on tokio; surfacing them to Bevy ECS requires a channel bridge (e.g. crossbeam or async_channel), adding 1-3 frame latency per event.
bevy_tokio_tasks crate exists but is unofficial, unmaintained since 2023, and does not cover libp2p's Swarm::select_next_some() poll model.
GossipSub message storms (common in P2P worlds) will saturate the tokio thread pool and starve Bevy's render thread if core affinity is not pinned manually.
mitigations: Isolate libp2p in a dedicated OS process; communicate via IPC (Unix socket / named pipe).
Use bevy_tokio_tasks fork and file a budget for maintenance.
Cap GossipSub fanout; implement backpressure before messages enter the ECS event queue.

hypothesis: Embedding a WebView (wry) inside a Bevy scene requires sharing a window handle. wgpu renders to the same HWND/NSView/GtkWidget that the WebView compositor also owns, causing undefined compositing behavior on all three major platforms.
evidence: wry uses WKWebView (macOS), WebView2 (Windows), WebKitGTK (Linux). Each has its own compositor (Metal, D3D11/D3D12, GL/Vulkan) that cannot share a GPU command queue with wgpu.
On macOS: WKWebView draws in a separate CALayer; wgpu targets a CAMetalLayer. Overlapping them without explicit layer ordering causes z-fighting and tearing at the OS compositor level.
On Windows: WebView2 uses a DXGI swap chain. wgpu also creates a DXGI swap chain. Two swap chains on the same HWND is explicitly unsupported by Microsoft (docs: 'only one swap chain per window').
On Linux: WebKitGTK requires a GtkWindow; Bevy's winit backend creates its own GDK/X11 surface. Nesting them requires a GtkPlug/GtkSocket hack that is deprecated in GTK4.
tauri v2 (which wraps wry) explicitly documents that mixing wgpu rendering inside a WebView window is unsupported and produces blank frames on Nvidia Wayland.
Alternative (offscreen WebView → texture) requires software rasterization fallback on most drivers, tanking GPU utilization by 40-70% based on chromium-headless benchmarks.
mitigations: Render WebView in a separate OS window, not inside the 3D scene.
Use offscreen rendering with explicit software-rasterization budget (accept the perf hit).
Replace embedded browser with an in-engine UI toolkit (bevy_egui or bevy_ui) for all UI needs.

hypothesis: SurrealDB embedded (RocksDB backend) runs GC and compaction on background threads; query latency is unbounded and will occasionally spike into the 10s of milliseconds, causing frame drops in a 60fps game loop.
evidence: SurrealDB 1.x embedded mode uses RocksDB for persistence. RocksDB level-compaction pauses have been measured at 5-80ms in write-heavy workloads (Facebook RocksDB tuning guide, 2023).
Bevy's fixed timestep is typically 1/60s = 16.67ms. A single 20ms compaction stall drops 1-2 frames visibly.

SurrealDB's query language (SurrealQL) is document/graph-oriented; Bevy ECS is an archetype-based columnar store. Synchronising entity state to SurrealDB requires serialisation on every write — O(n_components) per entity per dirty frame.
SurrealDB does not expose a zero-copy or memory-mapped API; all data crosses the serde boundary, adding allocation pressure inside the hot ECS update loop.
SurrealDB 1.x has no official Bevy integration; community crates (bevy_surreal) are prototype-quality (< 100 GitHub stars, last commit 2024-01).
Graph queries over a live 3D world (spatial adjacency, LOD dependency) run on the same embedded instance that receives writes from the ECS, causing read/write lock contention.
mitigations: Move SurrealDB to an out-of-process daemon; use async IPC for queries (decouples GC pauses from render loop).
Write to SurrealDB only on scene save / checkpoint events, not every ECS tick.
Use Bevy's ReflectComponent + RON/MessagePack for hot-path state; sync to SurrealDB lazily.

hypothesis: P2P architectures cannot deliver consistent <100ms latency for 3D world state synchronisation beyond ~50 peers without a relay/CDN layer, and NAT traversal success rates fall below 60% on typical consumer networks.
evidence: libp2p's hole-punching success rate: IPFS/libp2p team measured 58-72% success for TCP hole-punching, 70-82% for QUIC, in their 2022 network study (github.com/libp2p/go-libp2p, RFM17).

The remaining 20-40% of peers require a relay (Circuit Relay v2). Relay bandwidth is paid by the relay operator — in a P2P world, this is either the developer's cost or a tragedy-of-the-commons problem.

3D asset streaming: a 100MB scene chunk at 10Mbps consumer upload = 80 seconds to seed to one peer. With 10 peers, naive replication is 800s. BitTorrent-style chunking helps but adds implementation complexity.
GossipSub propagation latency for a 50-node mesh: median 120-250ms (libp2p testground benchmarks, 2023). This exceeds the 100ms threshold at which users perceive avatar lag.
Bevy's transform system runs at 60fps; at 250ms P2P latency, entity positions are 15 frames stale — interpolation is required but not provided by any current bevy+libp2p crate.
WebRTC DataChannel (an alternative) has better NAT traversal but libp2p-webrtc is experimental and not stable in the Rust implementation as of libp2p 0.53.
mitigations: Adopt a hybrid model: P2P for small lobbies (<16 peers), authoritative relay server for larger worlds.
Use content-addressed storage (IPFS/Iroh) for asset delivery; reserve P2P gossip for delta state only.
Implement client-side interpolation and lag compensation from day one.

hypothesis: Bevy has no stable 1.0 API. Major releases (every 3-4 months) routinely introduce breaking changes across core crates, making long-term platform investment extremely risky.
evidence: Bevy 0.13 (Feb 2024) → 0.14 (Jul 2024) → 0.15 (Dec 2024): each release required mechanical API migrations across rendering, ECS scheduling, asset system, and UI — all documented in official migration guides averaging 2,000+ words.
The bevy_render crate changed its RenderApp extraction API in 0.12, 0.13, and 0.14 — three consecutive breaks in 12 months for a core rendering concept.
No LTS release policy exists. The Bevy team has explicitly stated (RFC discussions, 2023) that stability guarantees are post-1.0 only, with no ETA for 1.0.
Third-party crate ecosystem is fragile: bevy_rapier (physics), bevy_egui (UI), bevy_kira_audio all lag 1-2 major Bevy versions, creating a 'dependency hell' window after each release.
A production platform (digital twin) requires multi-year stability. Unity LTS is 2 years; Unreal maintains backward compatibility across major versions for shipped titles.
mitigations: Pin to a specific Bevy version and budget 1-2 engineer-weeks per major Bevy upgrade.
Isolate Bevy behind an internal abstraction layer (engine facade pattern) to limit migration surface area.
Track Bevy RFC process and participate early to influence APIs the platform depends on.

hypothesis: Bevy lacks features that Godot, Unity, and Unreal ship out-of-the-box, forcing the team to build foundational infrastructure that competitors provide for free.
evidence: Godot 4.3: built-in multiplayer (ENet + WebRTC), SceneTree replication, physics (Jolt), animation state machines, terrain, NavMesh. Bevy has none of these as stable first-party crates.
Unity Netcode for GameObjects + Unity Relay provides NAT traversal + authoritative server relay with 1 line of config. Equivalent Bevy stack requires libp2p + custom relay + custom state sync.
Unreal's Chaos physics, Nanite geometry streaming, and Lumen GI are production-proven for large-scale 3D worlds. Bevy's renderer (wgpu-based) lacks Nanite-equivalent mesh LOD and has no GI solution.
Unity and Unreal both have digital twin / AEC integrations (Unity Reflect, Unreal Datasmith) with CAD format importers (IFC, FBX, STEP). Bevy has no CAD importer ecosystem.
Godot's GDExtension C API is stable across minor versions. Bevy's plugin API breaks every minor version (see R05).
Hiring: Unity/Unreal engineers are abundant. Bevy engineers are a tiny talent pool (estimated <5,000 active Bevy developers globally vs. 1.5M Unity developers).
mitigations: Scope Bevy to rendering/ECS only; use Godot as a comparison benchmark for feature completeness.
Evaluate Godot 4 seriously as an alternative — it has built-in P2P-adjacent networking and is MIT licensed.
If Bevy is chosen for its Rust safety guarantees, document the explicit build-vs-buy decisions and staff accordingly.
